sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 35, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 34 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 1.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see the current state more clearly:
- Left square: rows 39-43, cols 19-23 (SSSSS)
- Right square: rows 44-48, cols 34-38 (SSSSS)
Looking at rows 44-48:
`BBBBBBBBBBBBBBYYYOOSSSSSBBBBBBBBBBBB`
- BBBBBBBBBBBBBB: cols 15-28 (wall)
- YYY: cols 29-31 (passage)
- OO: cols 32-33 (passage)
- SSSSS: cols 34-38 (right square)
- BBBBBBBBBBBB: cols 39-50 (wall)
So the right square moved from cols 39-43 to cols 34-38 after pressing RIGHT. This is a LEFT movement (cols decreased from 39-43 to 34-38).
Wait, I think I was confused about the direction. Let me reconsider. The right square was at cols 39-43, and after pressing RIGHT, it moved to cols 34-38. This is a movement of -5 columns (to the left).
But that's weird - pressing RIGHT should move the square to the right, not the left. Unless... the RIGHT command moves the square towards the center of the maze?
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the column indices. Looking at the ascii:
- Before RIGHT: right square at cols 39-43
- After RIGHT: right square at cols 34-38
The right square moved from cols 39-43 to cols 34-38. In terms of absolute column indices, this is a movement to the LEFT (lower column numbers). But in terms of the game's coordinate system, maybe RIGHT means something different?
Actually, I think I need to reconsider. Maybe the RIGHT command doesn't move the square to the right in absolute coordinates, but rather moves it in the direction of the passage. Looking at the maze structure:
- The right square is in a chamber on the right side of the maze.
- The passage to the left of the right square is at cols 29-33 (YYYOO).
- The wall to the right of the right square is at cols 44-50.
So when I press RIGHT, the right square moves... to the left? That doesn't make sense.
Wait, let me re-examine. Maybe I misread the segmentation. Let me check the segmentation again.
Actually, looking at the segmentation output:
- After RIGHT: right square at cols 34-38
But looking at the ascii:
`BBBBBBBBBBBBBBYYYOOSSSSSBBBBBBBBBBBB`
- YYY: cols 29-31
- OO: cols 32-33
- SSSSS: cols 34-38
So the right square is at cols 34-38, which is to the RIGHT of the passage (cols 29-33). This means the right square moved from cols 39-43 to cols 34-38, which is a movement of -5 columns (to the left).
Hmm, this is confusing. Let me just try pressing RIGHT again to see what happens.
Actually, wait. I think I finally understand. The RIGHT command moves the square towards the RIGHT side of the maze. But the right square is already on the right side, so it moves... towards the center? No, that doesn't make sense.
Let me try a different approach. Let me just try pressing LEFT to see if the squares move in the opposite direction.
Actually, I think the key insight is that the squares move towards the center of the maze when pressing RIGHT or LEFT. The left square at cols 19-23 is to the left of the center (cols 29-33), so pressing RIGHT would move it towards the center (to the right). But the left square didn't move, which means it's blocked.
The right square at cols 39-43 is to the right of the center (cols 29-33), so pressing RIGHT would move it... away from the center? No, that doesn't make sense either.
OK let me just try more actions to understand the pattern. Let me press RIGHT again.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Press RIGHT again to see what happens
result = action(['RIGHT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  Sky blue: row=[{min(rows)},{max(rows)}], col=[{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")
print(f"Board changed: {last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Sky blue: row=[39,43], col=[19,23]
  Sky blue: row=[44,48], col=[34,38]
Board changed: False
